ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for Som Tam; A0CKP (mixed vegetable salad) used as closest defensible anchor. Specific Thai papaya salad code not available in FoodEx2 as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Thai green papaya salad weighs approximately 300 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as sold at Singaporean Thai food stalls and delivery platforms.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Papaya, raw (FDC ID 169926); cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table for composite Thai salad estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations / INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Som Tam as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central raw papaya (FDC ID 169926) as the dominant ingredient, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table for regional composite salad analogues. Input values from the original record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were used as a starting guide and validated against USDA and FAO/INFOODS ranges. Sodium at 300 mg/100g is consistent with fish sauce and dried shrimp contributions documented in USDA ingredient data. Energy (73 kcal/100g), protein (2.7 g), fat (3.3 g), and carbohydrate (8.3 g) are within the plausible range for this composite preparation. Values are treated as an estimated composite and confidence is set to medium accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Som Tam (Thai green papaya salad) found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on: (1) raw unripe green papaya GI approximately 40–50 per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables for raw papaya analogues; (2) palm sugar dressing raises effective GI modestly; (3) dietary fibre at 1.7 g/100g and acidity from lime juice attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × available carbohydrate per serving (300 g × 8.3 g/100g = 24.9 g) / 100 = 11.2. Classified as low GI (less than 55) and moderate GL (10–19). Atkinson et al. 2021 is a peer-reviewed publication in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition and constitutes an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g driven by fish sauce and dried shrimp; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 900 mg sodium, representing 39–45% of the WHO/DASH daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and omit or minimise dried shrimp to lower sodium intake; choosing a reduced-sodium fish sauce variant can reduce per-serving sodium by approximately 30–40%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Palm sugar in the dressing contributes 5.0 g sugar per 100 g with only 1.7 g dietary fibre per 100 g; estimated GI of 45 (low) is favourable but the moderate glycemic load of 11.2 per serving warrants portion awareness.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a half-plate portion (150 g) to keep glycemic load below 6, and should request less palm sugar in the dressing to reduce added sugar and postprandial glucose impact per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 0.5 g per 100 g and trans fat is 0.0 g, which is favourable; however, sodium at 300 mg per 100 g (900 mg per serving) is a significant cardiovascular risk factor, and peanuts contribute unsaturated fats that are cardioprotective.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced fish sauce to manage sodium load, while benefiting from the cardioprotective MUFA/PUFA content of peanuts and the antioxidant vitamin C (20 mg per 100 g) from green papaya and lime juice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (20 mg/100g) from green papaya and lime juice, anti-inflammatory polyphenols from garlic, and MUFA/PUFA from peanuts support anti-inflammatory dietary patterns; nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should request tomato and chilli to be reduced or omitted, and should limit dried shrimp to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Green Papaya Salad (Som Tam; ส้มตำ; 青木瓜沙拉) is a vibrant, refreshing, and spicy Southeast Asian salad originating in Thailand and widely served across Singapore at Thai food stalls and via delivery platforms. The dish is prepared by pounding unripe shredded green papaya together with long beans, cherry tomatoes, dried shrimp, garlic, and chilli padi in a traditional mortar and pestle, then dressing the mixture with fish sauce, lime juice, and palm sugar to create a complex balance of sour, salty, sweet, and spicy flavours. The texture is crisp and crunchy from the raw papaya and long beans, with a tangy and aromatic dressing. Peanuts are typically added for richness and a nutty finish. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 73 kcal per 100 g, with 2.7 g protein, 3.3 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from peanuts), and 8.3 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 300 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce and dried shrimp, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The estimated glycemic index is 45 (low), supported by the raw unripe papaya base and lime juice acidity, though palm sugar in the dressing raises the sugar content to 5.0 g per 100 g. Vitamin C is notable at 20 mg per 100 g, contributing antioxidant benefit relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and immune health. The dish is halal and gluten-free but contains peanut, fish, and shellfish allergens. A standard serving is one plate (300 g), delivering approximately 219 kcal and 900 mg sodium.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Som Tam as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central raw papaya data and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table. GI is an analogous estimate from the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is medium because the composite dish nutrient profile is estimated rather than directly measured from an approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Som Tam composite dish not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish not reported in the approved sources consulted. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Thai salad preparation not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for raw vegetable and peanut-based preparations with no hydrogenated fats. Nutrient source updated from iteration 2: replaced HPB Foodo as co-primary source with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table, which is an approved source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. The input record URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a nutrient evidence source; it was noted in the input as a menu listing reference only. GI source is Atkinson et al. 2021, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal), which is an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2. Halal status retained from input cultural tags; gluten-free status retained as no gluten-containing ingredients are present in the standard preparation. Nightshade caution for rheumatoid arthritis documented in additional_conditions.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Added mandatory Thai local-language name (ส้มตำ), Chinese script (青木瓜沙拉), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names; expanded aliases and aliases_by_language for multilingual compliance.

Iteration 2: Updated FoodEx2 match type to closest_parent; clarified nutrient_source to reference USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore; added GI source from Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed journal.

Updated gi_source notes to explicitly confirm Atkinson et al. 2021 American Journal of Clinical Nutrition is an approved peer-reviewed GI source. Updated notes to reflect source changes and document all -1 fields against the corrected approved sources.
